source.title: Compounds identified by virtual docking to a tetrameric EGFR extracellular domain can modulate Grb2 internalization

gist_memory.paper_problem_memory: EGFR overexpression/mutation drives many solid tumors, but resistance to current therapies (antibodies targeting extracellular domain, kinase inhibitors) necessitates new mechanisms. The paper hypothesizes that stabilizing a prone tetrameric EGFR extracellular domain conformation can modulate signaling and internalization, offering a novel drug target.

section_gists.heading: Abstract
section_gists.gist: Summarizes the problem (EGFR-driven tumors, resistance), approach (virtual docking to tetramer interface, Grb2 internalization screen), key results (14 hits modulate Grb2 internalization, limited effect on viability/phosphorylation, some synergy with cetuximab/erlotinib), and conclusion (tetrameric EGFR is a novel target).
process_signals: Problem: EGFR overexpression/mutation drives tumors; resistance to current therapies.
Hypothesis: Prone tetrameric EGFR configuration is a potential drug target.
Approach: Virtual screening of 345,232 compounds against tetramer interface; 109 selected; cell-based Grb2 internalization screen.
Key results: 14 hits affect Grb2 internalization; most have limited effect on viability and phosphorylation; some potentiate cetuximab/erlotinib.
Limitation: No direct binding confirmation.
failures_or_limits: Variable effects on viability and phosphorylation

section_gists.heading: Background
section_gists.gist: Details EGFR's role in cancer, resistance mechanisms, and current therapies. Introduces the concept of EGFR oligomerization, particularly the prone head-to-head tetramer, and hypothesizes that stabilizing this tetramer could disrupt signaling. Sets the stage for targeting the tetramer interface with small molecules.
process_signals: Problem: EGFR overexpression/mutation drives tumors; resistance via EGFRvIII, T790M, etc.
Constraint: Need for new mechanisms; combination therapies beneficial.
Hypothesis: Prone tetramer has unique interface; stabilizing it may modulate signaling/internalization.
Prior knowledge: Tetramers exist; head-to-head configuration proposed; high-affinity EGF binding may involve tetramer.
Goal: Identify compounds that influence EGFR internalization via tetramer stabilization.

section_gists.heading: Virtual docking and in silico compound selection
section_gists.gist: Describes computational design: tetramer model from MD simulation, three docking boxes at the interface, library of ~350,000 drug-like compounds filtered by solubility (LogS > -6.0), prepared with LigPrep (tautomers, ionization states), docked with Glide SP. Top 15,000 manually filtered for diversity, 109 selected for purchase.
process_signals: Tetramer model: theoretical prone head-to-head from Winn lab, includes EGF.
Docking boxes: three overlapping boxes at interface; Box 2 includes EGF, Boxes 1 and 3 flank it.
Library: Life Chemicals ~350,000 compounds; filtered for drug-likeness, LogS > -6.0.
Docking: Glide SP; ~1.5 million docking processes.
Selection: Top 15,000 manually filtered for chemical diversity and interaction with discrete elements; 109 purchased.
Constraint: Computational limitations prevented docking entire interface at once.
methods: Protein preparation: Maestro Protein Preparation Wizard
failures_or_limits: Computational limitations forced division into three boxes

section_gists.heading: Grb2 internalization assay and compound selection metrics
section_gists.gist: Details the primary screening assay: SCC61 cells, serum-starved, pretreated with compounds (10 µM) for 5 min, then EGF (10 ng/ml) for 50 min. Grb2 pit formation imaged and quantified. Controls: cetuximab (2 µM), erlotinib (20 µM). Hit selection based on pit count ratio (+EGF/-EGF) and FDR. Plates with ratio <1.5 excluded.
process_signals: Assay: Grb2 internalization as surrogate for EGFR activity; time points based on recycling kinetics.
Cell line: SCC61 (head and neck SCC, high EGFR).
Compound concentration: 10 µM.
Controls: cetuximab (2 µM), erlotinib (20 µM).
Hit selection: normalized pit count ratio; FDR ranking.
Quality control: plates with EGF/untreated ratio <1.5 excluded.
metrics: Sum of pit count per well

section_gists.heading: In silico selection of compounds targeting a prone EGFR tetramer
section_gists.gist: Rationale: tetramer-specific interface could bind small molecules to stabilize tetramer. Docking performed on three boxes. From 345,232 compounds, after filtering and tautomer expansion (496,398 ligands), top 15,000 manually selected for diversity, 109 purchased. All docked within 4Å of both EGFR molecules; 27 also within 4Å of EGF. None similar to known kinase inhibitors.
process_signals: Hypothesis: Ligand binding at interface stabilizes tetramer, increases mole fraction, promotes function.
Docking boxes: one includes EGF, two flank.
Library size: 345,232 -> 496,398 with tautomers.
Selection: manual filtering of top 15,000 for diversity; 109 chosen.
Result: all 109 dock within 4Å of both EGFR chains; 27 near EGF.
Novelty: compounds not similar to erlotinib.

section_gists.heading: Candidate compounds alter internalization of the EGFR effector Grb2
section_gists.gist: Grb2 internalization assay used as surrogate for EGFR activity. 14 hits identified, classified into 4 classes based on pit count changes ±EGF. Examples: F0922-0900 (Class 1, reduces pits with EGF), F2738-2186 (Class 2, increases pits with EGF). Controls cetuximab and erlotinib reduced pits. Hits selected based on phenotypic robustness and predicted EGF interaction.
process_signals: Assay rationale: EGFR internalization involves Grb2; stabilization of tetramer may alter internalization.
Hit classification: 4 classes based on pit count changes ±EGF.
14 hits selected for further analysis.
Controls: cetuximab and erlotinib reduce pits as expected.
Selection criteria: phenotypic robustness and docked location (EGF interaction).

section_gists.heading: Compound effect on cell viability
section_gists.gist: 14 hits tested for viability in SCC61 and A431 cells. In SCC61, only F2738-2186 reduced viability (25%); F5230-0424 modestly potentiated erlotinib. In A431, erlotinib and cetuximab reduced viability, but no hit alone did; F3385-3143 increased viability. Some hits modestly enhanced erlotinib or cetuximab effects.
process_signals: Viability assay: 72h treatment, 10 µM compounds, 2 µM erlotinib, 4 µM cetuximab.
SCC61: most hits no effect; F2738-2186 reduced viability; F5230-0424 + erlotinib modest synergy.
A431: erlotinib/cetuximab effective; no hit alone reduced viability; F3385-3143 increased viability.
Synergy: some hits modestly enhanced erlotinib or cetuximab in A431.
Limitation: effects not consistent across cell lines.
failures_or_limits: Most hits did not affect viability alone
Synergy modest and not always significant

section_gists.heading: EGFR-Y1068, EGFR-Y1173, and ERK1/2-T202/Y204 phosphorylation in response to compound treatment
section_gists.gist: 6 hits tested for phosphorylation effects in SCC61. Erlotinib blocked all EGF-induced phosphorylation; cetuximab slightly elevated Y1173. Two Class 2 hits (F2738-2186, F2573-0380) also slightly elevated Y1173; F0922-0900 slightly reduced it. No significant effects on Y1068. F2573-0380 slightly increased basal ERK1/2 phosphorylation.
process_signals: Phosphorylation assay: 1h pretreatment, 45min EGF, Western blot.
Erlotinib: complete inhibition.
Cetuximab: slight increase in Y1173.
Hits: minimal effects; some slight modulation of Y1173.
No consistent pattern across hits.
Limitation: small subset tested; effects not robust.
failures_or_limits: Most hits did not significantly affect phosphorylation

section_gists.heading: Analysis of hit compounds
section_gists.gist: Summary of hit data. 4/14 hits from subset predicted to interact with EGF. Docking poses show common interaction with Arg470 or neighboring residues (Ser468, Asn469, etc.). EGF-interacting hits contact EGF residues Lys48-Leu52.
process_signals: Hit enrichment: 4/14 from EGF-interacting subset (28% of library).
Common docking feature: Arg470 or nearby residues.
EGF interaction region: Lys48-Leu52.
Suggests binding mode hypothesis.

section_gists.heading: Discussion
section_gists.gist: Interprets results: 14/109 hits is high hit rate, supporting tetramer model. Compounds modulate Grb2 internalization with little effect on phosphorylation, similar to cetuximab. Some potentiate clinical inhibitors. Mechanism: stabilization of tetramer may shift equilibrium, altering function. Limitations: no direct binding evidence; need for target engagement studies; unknown binding mode; potential off-target effects. Future: optimization, binding studies, in vivo models.
process_signals: High hit rate (14/109) supports tetramer model.
Mechanism hypothesis: stabilization of tetramer alters quaternary equilibrium.
Similarity to cetuximab: both target domain III but different conformations; combination possible.
Limitations: no direct binding proof; unknown if compounds bind as predicted; need for further optimization.
Future directions: target engagement studies, chemical optimization, in vivo testing.

section_gists.heading: Conclusion
section_gists.gist: Virtual screening targeting tetramer-specific cavities combined with cell-based screen identified compounds modulating EGFR function via a novel mechanism. Binding not confirmed, but Grb2-tetramer interaction supports hypothesis.
process_signals: Main finding: novel compounds modulate EGFR function, presumably via tetramer stabilization.
Limitation: binding not experimentally confirmed.
Validation: Grb2 specifically interacts with tetrameric EGFR, supporting design.

global_hypotheses_about_storyline: The research evolved from the problem of EGFR therapy resistance to the hypothesis that stabilizing a prone tetramer conformation could modulate signaling/internalization.
Computational docking was used to identify candidate stabilizers, followed by a cell-based Grb2 internalization screen as a functional readout.
The high hit rate and functional effects support the tetramer model, but lack of direct binding evidence leaves the mechanism unconfirmed.
The study represents an early-stage discovery effort, with hits requiring further optimization and validation.
uncertainties_to_resolve: Do the compounds actually bind to the tetramer interface as predicted?
What is the exact binding mode and affinity?
Are the effects on Grb2 internalization directly due to tetramer stabilization?
Can the compounds be optimized for potency and selectivity?
Would in vivo models show efficacy?

events.summary: EGFR overexpression/mutation drives tumors, and resistance to current therapies motivates new mechanisms.
The prone EGFR tetramer contains a novel protein-protein interface that can be targeted for stabilization by small molecules.
Virtual screening of 345,232 drug-like compounds against the tetramer interface using three docking boxes.
Top 15,000 compounds manually filtered for chemical diversity; 109 selected for purchase.
Grb2 internalization assay used as primary screen: SCC61 cells, 10 µM compounds, 5 min pretreatment, 50 min EGF stimulation.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk covers abstract, background, and methods sections, providing problem statement, hypothesis, computational design, and experimental assay details.

events.summary: Design of Grb2 internalization assay as surrogate for EGFR activity
Hypothesis that small-molecule stabilization of EGFR tetramer affects internalization
Primary screen identifies 14 hit compounds altering Grb2 pit formation
Viability assay of 14 hits in SCC61 and A431 cells
Phosphorylation analysis of 6 hits in SCC61 cells
Docking pose analysis reveals common interaction with Arg470

chunk_events.notes: Chunk covers experimental methods and results for Grb2 internalization screen, viability, phosphorylation, and docking analysis. Key events include assay design, hit identification, and limitations.

events.summary: 14 preliminary hit compounds were identified from the Grb2 internalization screen, classified into 4 classes based on pit count changes ±EGF.
Cell viability assays in SCC61 and A431 cells showed that most hits did not affect viability alone; F2738-2186 reduced SCC61 viability by 25%.
Phosphorylation analysis of EGFR and ERK1/2 in SCC61 cells showed that hits had minimal effects; erlotinib blocked all EGF-induced phosphorylation.
Docking pose analysis revealed that 4 of 6 evaluated hits dock to Arg-470, and EGF-interacting hits contact EGF residues Lys48-Leu52.
numbers_or_metrics: 2 of 6 predicted to interact with EGF

events.summary: The mechanism hypothesis is that compounds stabilize the prone tetramer, shifting quaternary equilibrium and altering EGFR function.
The study design combined virtual docking to the tetramer interface with a cell-based Grb2 internalization screen to identify functional modulators.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk covers key experimental results (Grb2 screen, viability, phosphorylation), docking analysis, and discussion of hit rate and limitations.

events.summary: Validation of Grb2 internalization as readout for EGFR tetramer stabilization by biophysical analysis.
numbers_or_metrics: 95% of tetramers contain at least one bound Grb2

events.summary: Limitation: Grb2 screening assay does not diagnose mechanism; compounds may stabilize, destabilize, or alter oligomeric structure.
Future work: medicinal chemical optimization and structure-activity relationships to validate mode of action.
Future work: explore effect on mobilization of total and phosphorylated EGFR and other effector pathways.
Conclusion: virtual screening targeting tetramer-specific cavities combined with cell-based screen identifies novel compounds modulating EGFR function.
Limitation: current studies do not establish whether active compounds bind to EGFR tetramer as predicted by docking.
Validation: demonstration that Grb2 specifically interacts with tetrameric EGFR supports experimental design hypotheses.

chunk_events.notes: This chunk contains the conclusion and discussion, highlighting validation of the Grb2 readout, limitations of the screening assay, and future directions. It reinforces the paper's main findings and uncertainties.
This chunk contains only references and a submission advertisement, with no research process events.

evidence_bank.quote: Overexpression or mutation of the epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) potently enhances the growth of many solid tumors.
Tumor cells frequently display resistance to mechanistically-distinct EGFR-directed therapeutic agents, making it valuable to develop therapeutics that work by additional mechanisms.
Recent studies have identified a novel prone extracellular tetrameric EGFR configuration, which we identify as a potential target for drug discovery.

Our focus is on the prone EGFR tetramer, which contains a novel protein-protein interface involving extracellular domain III. This EGFR tetramer is computationally targeted for stabilization by small molecule ligand binding.

This study performed virtual screening of a Life Chemicals, Inc. small molecule library of 345,232 drug-like compounds against a molecular dynamics simulation of protein-protein interfaces distinct to the novel tetramer.
An estimate of solubility (LogS) was generated for each compound using QikProp and compounds were filtered using a cutoff of -6.0 for LogS.

All compounds were then viewed as docked on the receptor and manual selection was based on docking position to maximize diversity of docking locations on the EGFR dimer. One hundred nine compounds were selected for functional evaluation
After 5 min, EGF (10 ng/ml in serum free media) or vehicle was added. After 50 min, cells were washed once with PBS, and fixed for ten minutes at room temperature in 4 % PFA.
We developed a high-content fluorescence microscopy-based screen to assess candidate compound activity in influencing internalization of the EGFR effector Grb2 in SCC61 cells
We chose to use Grb2 as a surrogate marker because it was unclear how EGFR activity state, reflected by auto-phosphorylation, would be affected by addition of compounds
We posited that targeted small-molecule stabilization of the EGFR tetramer would also affect internalization and recycling.

Fourteen preliminary hit compounds were selected for further analysis, based on phenotypic robustness as well as the combination of phenotype and whether or not the docked location predicted interaction with EGF.
Compounds that alter Grb2 positive pit formation could fall into one of 4 possible classes.
Viability in response to compound alone or compounds in combination with erlotinib or cetuximab was determined with the CellTiterBlue assay
For these experiments, lower doses of cetuximab and erlotinib were used than for screening, to maximize the chance of identifying synergy with newly identified compounds.
These time points were taken to parallel the process of EGFR/Grb2 recycling, and correspond to time periods known to reflect maximal changes in activity following EGF stimulation
All purchased compounds docked within 4Å of both EGFR molecules; 27 also docked within 4Å of at least one EGF molecule
14 of 109 compounds predicted to stabilize a recently defined homo-tetramer of EGFR influenced intracellular trafficking of the EGFR effector Grb2.
Typical screen results for the 14 preliminary hit compounds are shown in Fig. 2d
no test compound significantly reduced the viability of A431 cells
Each of these EGF-induced phosphorylation events was entirely blocked by treatment with the EGFR kinase inhibitor erlotinib.
4 of the 6 hits dock to Arg-470, and the 2 that are not predicted to interact with this residue, are predicted to interact with neighboring residues
the putative EGF interactions all involve EGF residues in the region of Lys48-Leu52

Using the unique prone tetramer of EGFR as a target, we deduced that stabilization of the head-to-head interface could potentially alter EGFR function by shifting the equilibrium toward this tetramer.
14 of 109 compounds predicted to stabilize a recently defined homo-tetramer of EGFR influenced intracellular trafficking of the EGFR effector Grb2.
2 % of EGFR is monomeric, 5 % is dimeric, <1 % is trimeric, and 94 % is tetrameric.
nearly 95 % of the EGFR tetramers containing at least one bound Grb2

identification of compounds in the Grb2 screening assay is not diagnostic of the mechanism of action of the compound; it is formally possible that the compounds are stabilizing, destabilizing, or not affecting stability, but rather altering EGFR oligomeric structure

It will be important to perform classic medicinal chemical optimization, and establishment of structure-activity relationships in relation to the docking boxes to validate the mode of action of compounds emerging as hits.

further investigation of the compounds identified in this screen should expand to explore their effect on the mobilization of total and phosphorylated (active) EGFR, and other EGFR-dependent effector pathways.

Our current study demonstrates that focusing on EGFR tetramer-specific surface cavities as targets for virtual screening, combined with a high content cell-based screening approach, can identify novel compounds that modulate EGFR function, presumably via a novel mechanism.
While the current studies do not establish whether the active compounds bind to the EGFR tetramer as predicted by docking
the cited demonstration that Grb2 specifically interacts with a tetrameric form of EGFR provides an exciting validation to hypotheses that drove our experimental design.

We developed a high-content fluorescence microscopy-based screen to assess candidate compound activity in influencing internalization of the EGFR effector Grb2 in SCC61 cells, a head and neck squamous cell carcinoma cell line that expresses abundant EGFR [39].
Based on this analysis, 109 candidate ligands were selected for purchase and in vitro analysis.

While most studies have addressed the protein packing events associated with tetrameric conformations defined as “back-to-back” or “side-to-side”, in which EGFR protrudes at a 90° angle to the cell membrane, an intriguing group of studies have supported the existence of a physiological “head-to-head” tetramer, in which the extracellular domains of EGFR lie prone along the plasma membrane (Fig.
These pre-formed dimers and multimers have been associated with specific activity states of EGFR (discussed in $[18]$ ).

Dimeric EGFR binding to two molecules of EGF is characterized by high-affinity and low-affinity events, which are proposed to represent an initial high-affinity binding event that favors an asymmetric dimeric structure in which the second subunit is sterically prevented from binding ligand [12].

event_graph.paper_main_problem: EGFR overexpression/mutation drives many solid tumors, but resistance to current therapies (antibodies targeting extracellular domain, kinase inhibitors) necessitates new mechanisms. The paper hypothesizes that stabilizing a prone tetrameric EGFR extracellular domain conformation can modulate signaling and internalization, offering a novel drug target.

canonical_events.summary: EGFR overexpression/mutation drives tumors, and resistance to current therapies motivates new mechanisms.
The prone EGFR tetramer contains a novel protein-protein interface that can be targeted for stabilization by small molecules.
Virtual screening of 345,232 drug-like compounds against the tetramer interface using three docking boxes.
Top 15,000 compounds manually filtered for chemical diversity; 109 selected for purchase.
Design of Grb2 internalization assay as surrogate for EGFR activity.
Primary screen identifies 14 hit compounds altering Grb2 pit formation, classified into 4 phenotypic classes.
Viability assay of 14 hits in SCC61 and A431 cells: most hits no effect alone; F2738-2186 reduced SCC61 viability by 25%; some modest synergy with erlotinib/cetuximab.
Phosphorylation analysis of 6 hits in SCC61 cells: erlotinib blocked all EGF-induced phosphorylation; hits had minimal effects; cetuximab slightly elevated Y1173.
Docking pose analysis reveals common interaction with Arg470; EGF-interacting hits contact EGF residues Lys48-Leu52.
key_numbers: 2 of 6 predicted to interact with EGF

canonical_events.summary: Mechanism hypothesis: compounds stabilize the prone tetramer, shifting quaternary equilibrium and altering EGFR function.
Validation of Grb2 internalization as readout: biophysical analysis shows 94% of EGFR is tetrameric, and 95% of tetramers contain at least one bound Grb2.
key_numbers: 95% of tetramers contain at least one bound Grb2

canonical_events.summary: Limitation: Grb2 screening assay does not diagnose mechanism; compounds may stabilize, destabilize, or alter oligomeric structure.
Limitation: current studies do not establish whether active compounds bind to EGFR tetramer as predicted by docking.
Future work: medicinal chemical optimization and structure-activity relationships to validate mode of action; explore effects on EGFR mobilization and other effector pathways.

edges.reason: The problem of resistance motivates the hypothesis of targeting the tetramer interface.
The hypothesis leads to the design of virtual screening against the tetramer interface.
Virtual screening results in selection of 109 compounds for purchase.
Selected compounds are tested in the designed Grb2 internalization assay.
The assay design enables the primary screen identifying 14 hits.
Hits are further tested in viability assays.
Hits are further tested in phosphorylation assays.
Docking poses of hits are analyzed to identify common interactions.
Docking analysis supports the mechanism hypothesis of tetramer stabilization.
Biophysical validation supports the use of Grb2 internalization as a readout for tetramer stabilization.
The assay cannot distinguish between stabilization, destabilization, or other oligomeric changes.
No direct binding evidence confirms the predicted docking mode.
Limitations motivate future work on medicinal chemistry and target engagement studies.
Lack of binding confirmation motivates future work on validating mode of action.

must_cover_points: Hypothesis of targeting prone tetramer interface
Virtual screening design and compound selection
Viability and phosphorylation effects of hits
Docking pose analysis and common interactions
Limitations: no direct binding, unknown mechanism
Future directions for optimization and validation
